structuralPatterns.pattern: Conclusion echoes / restates the title or theme
structuralPatterns.description: The final 1-2 sentences loop back to the year's theme word(s), giving the essay a satisfying ring of completion and ensuring the examiner knows the topic was addressed.

examples.quote: ...proud of the teamwork we had displayed in working together to win the competition.
Tommy had changed for the better, and it all began with that simple history project.
Trying something new was not bad at all!

structuralPatterns.pattern: Opening uses an in-the-moment sensory hook or dialogue
structuralPatterns.description: Instead of starting with 'One sunny day', the essays begin right in the middle of a conversation, an action, or a sensory detail to immediately grab the reader's attention.

examples.quote: "What's this?" I asked, waving an envelope I had found on the dining table.
"Grrr..." Kim shot a knowing look at her sister Jolie.
"Have you set up an appointment yet?" I prodded my mother again as she sipped her coffee...

structuralPatterns.pattern: Five-paragraph narrative arc (Setup → Rising Action → Climax → Falling Action → Resolution)
structuralPatterns.description: The story builds logically. Paragraphs 1-2 set the scene and introduce the problem, paragraphs 3-4 contain the main event or climax, and the final paragraph resolves the issue.

examples.quote: Setup: going to mall. Rising: buying ice cream. Climax: wallet lost. Falling: info counter. Resolution: found it.
Setup: lazy boy. Rising: project and training. Climax: charity run. Resolution: changed for the better.

structuralPatterns.pattern: Lesson / reflection appears in the final paragraph (the takeaway)
structuralPatterns.description: The protagonist reflects on what they learned from the experience, adding emotional depth to the narrative rather than just ending the physical action.

examples.quote: It had special significance, for it was a symbol of my brother's love for me.
That simple train ride taught me that kindness does not need to be grand. Even giving up a seat can make a big difference.

structuralPatterns.pattern: Climax is centered on a sudden realisation, mishap, or reveal
structuralPatterns.description: The peak of the story usually involves a sudden shift in emotion—a shock, a mistake, or a surprise—that forces the character to react quickly.

examples.quote: She turned around and froze. It was Lisa's mother.
Suddenly, my cousin coughed and before I knew it, she had thrown up milk all over me!
Roger dug around his pocket for his wallet. To his horror, he was unable to find it.

tips.tip: Start with an '-ing' (participial) phrase to combine actions and avoid starting with 'I'.
tips.example: Boring: 'I felt bored and looked around.' Better: 'Feeling bored, I looked around for my aunt...' (2019)

tips.tip: Start with an adverb to immediately set the tone of the action.
tips.example: Boring: 'I opened it and wondered what was inside.' Better: 'I eagerly opened it, wondering what it could contain.' (2019)

tips.tip: Use a subordinate clause (As / When / After) to establish timing and flow.
tips.example: Boring: 'The car turned the corner. The view unfolded.' Better: 'As the car turned the corner, the scenic view of the sea unfolded before their eyes.' (2024)

tips.tip: Use time-markers to skip boring parts and move directly to the next important scene.
tips.example: Boring: 'We waited for a few weeks. Then it was my birthday.' Better: 'It so happened that my birthday was a few weeks after that.' (2017)

tips.tip: Use a summary of the previous action to launch the next paragraph smoothly.
tips.example: Boring: 'We got the information. Then I wrote the articles.' Better: 'After we had gathered all the information required, I proceeded to write the articles...' (2018)

tips.tip: Transition using a change in location or arrival to signal a new scene.
tips.example: Boring: 'We went to my aunt's house. It was noisy.' Better: 'Upon arriving at my aunt's house, we were greeted by raucous laughter...' (2019)

tips.tip: Show guilt or worry through facial expressions and fading voice.
tips.example: Boring: 'He looked guilty and stopped talking.' Better: 'He had a guilty expression on his face... his voice trailed off.' (2017)

tips.tip: Show excitement or impatience through physical movement and fidgeting.
tips.example: Boring: 'I was very impatient.' Better: 'I could hardly stay still in my seat... incessant pen-clicking, foot tapping and intermittent sighs.' (2022)

tips.tip: Show fear or shock through physical freezing or loss of color in the face.
tips.example: Boring: 'She was shocked to see her mother.' Better: 'She turned around and froze... her face was pale.' (2016)

craftCategories.category: Pacing — when to slow down and when to speed up

tips.tip: Speed up (summarize) routine days or long waits using a single sentence.
tips.example: Boring: 'On Monday I waited. On Tuesday I waited.' Better: 'School on Wednesday and Thursday passed by with the same restless energy.' (2022)

tips.tip: Slow down during the climax by describing step-by-step actions and internal panic.
tips.example: Boring: 'I couldn't find my wallet.' Better: 'Roger dug around his pocket for his wallet. To his horror, he was unable to find it. He searched his other pockets and peered into the shopping cart.' (2020)

tips.tip: Stretch out a moment of anticipation by focusing on the passage of time.
tips.example: Boring: 'School felt long.' Better: 'Seconds stretched into minutes and minutes stretched into hours.' (2022)

tips.tip: Use weather and sky descriptions to reflect the mood of the characters.
tips.example: Boring: 'It was a nice day.' Better: 'The clear blue sky was a welcome sight which lifted their mood even more.' (2024)

tips.tip: Engage the sense of smell and sound to make a location feel three-dimensional.
tips.example: Boring: 'The house was noisy and had food.' Better: '...greeted by raucous laughter and loud chatter... The aroma of food wafted in the air...' (2019 & 2024)

tips.tip: Describe the physical environment to show, not just tell, the state of a place.
tips.example: Boring: 'His room was messy.' Better: 'His room was a chaotic mess...' (2023)

tips.tip: End with a philosophical realization or lesson learned that applies to real life.
tips.example: Boring: 'I liked the train ride.' Better: 'That simple train ride taught me that kindness does not need to be grand.' (2025)

tips.tip: Revisit an object or action from the beginning of the story to create a 'full circle' ending.
tips.example: Boring: 'We went home.' Better: 'As the car drove off, Justin leaned against the window... He was also glad that he added lettuce into his sandwich...' (2024)

tips.tip: Explicitly state the theme word to leave no doubt for the examiner that you answered the prompt.
tips.example: Boring: 'He was a better person now.' Better: 'Tommy had changed for the better, and it all began with that simple history project.' (2023)
